# 09-flux-monorepo

## What this is
The canonical Flux CD layout promoted in the Flux documentation and the upstream
`fluxcd/flux2-kustomize-helm-example` repository. A single Git monorepo drives
one or more clusters. Work is split three ways: `apps/` holds the workloads (a
Kustomize base plus per-environment overlays), `infrastructure/` holds the
platform pieces every app depends on (ingress controller, cert-manager issuers),
and `clusters/<cluster>/` holds the per-cluster entry points that Flux
reconciles. `flux bootstrap` writes the `clusters/<cluster>/flux-system/`
directory (the `gotk-*` files) into the repo and commits it; everything else is
authored by humans. This shape is what most teams inherit when they adopt Flux
"the documented way," and it scales from one cluster to many by adding another
`clusters/<name>/` directory.

## Layout
```
09-flux-monorepo/
├── README.md
├── apps/
│   ├── base/
│   │   └── frontend/
│   │       ├── deployment.yaml
│   │       ├── service.yaml
│   │       └── kustomization.yaml        # kustomize.config.k8s.io (build file)
│   ├── staging/
│   │   ├── frontend-patch.yaml           # partial Deployment (patch)
│   │   └── kustomization.yaml            # kustomize.config.k8s.io overlay
│   └── production/
│       ├── frontend-patch.yaml           # partial Deployment (patch)
│       └── kustomization.yaml            # kustomize.config.k8s.io overlay
├── infrastructure/
│   ├── controllers/
│   │   ├── ingress-nginx.yaml            # HelmRepository + HelmRelease
│   │   └── kustomization.yaml            # kustomize.config.k8s.io (build file)
│   └── configs/
│       ├── cluster-issuers.yaml          # cert-manager ClusterIssuer
│       └── kustomization.yaml            # kustomize.config.k8s.io (build file)
└── clusters/
    ├── staging/
    │   ├── flux-system/                  # written by `flux bootstrap`
    │   │   ├── gotk-components.yaml       # TRUNCATED (~35k lines in reality)
    │   │   ├── gotk-sync.yaml             # GitRepository + root Kustomization CR
    │   │   └── kustomization.yaml         # kustomize.config.k8s.io (build file)
    │   ├── apps.yaml                      # Flux Kustomization CR (NOT a build file)
    │   ├── infrastructure.yaml            # Flux Kustomization CRs (NOT build files)
    │   └── kustomization.yaml             # kustomize.config.k8s.io (build file)
    └── production/
        ├── flux-system/
        │   ├── gotk-components.yaml       # TRUNCATED (~35k lines in reality)
        │   ├── gotk-sync.yaml             # GitRepository + root Kustomization CR
        │   └── kustomization.yaml         # kustomize.config.k8s.io (build file)
        ├── apps.yaml                      # Flux Kustomization CR (NOT a build file)
        ├── infrastructure.yaml            # Flux Kustomization CRs (NOT build files)
        └── kustomization.yaml             # kustomize.config.k8s.io (build file)
```

## What makes it structurally distinct
- **Two unrelated kinds share the word "Kustomization."** Every file literally
  named `kustomization.yaml` is a `kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1` Kustomization:
  a *build instruction* that `kustomize`/the kustomize-controller reads to render
  manifests. The API server never stores one. By contrast, `clusters/*/apps.yaml`
  and `clusters/*/infrastructure.yaml` contain
  `kustomize.toolkit.fluxcd.io/v1` Kustomizations: *custom resources* the API
  server stores and the kustomize-controller reconciles. Same word, different
  `apiVersion`, different lifecycle, different filename convention. The Flux CR is
  never in a file called `kustomization.yaml`, and the build file is never named
  `apps.yaml`.
- **`clusters/<cluster>/flux-system/` is the strongest Flux fingerprint.** The
  trio `gotk-components.yaml`, `gotk-sync.yaml`, `kustomization.yaml` with the
  `# This file is generated by flux bootstrap. DO NOT EDIT.` header is written by
  `flux bootstrap` and appears in no other tool's layout. `gotk-sync.yaml`
  contains the `GitRepository`/`Kustomization` pair named `flux-system` whose
  `path` points back at this same cluster directory — the reconciliation loop's
  root.
- **`apps/` vs `infrastructure/` is a reconciliation-ordering split, not just
  tidiness.** The Flux Kustomization for `apps` sets `dependsOn: [infrastructure]`
  and the one for `infra-configs` sets `dependsOn: [infrastructure]`, so
  controllers (ingress-nginx, cert-manager CRDs) reconcile and pass their
  `healthChecks` before configs and before any application object is applied. The
  directory boundary encodes an ordering guarantee.
- **The cluster root is a Kustomization listing Kustomizations.**
  `clusters/production/kustomization.yaml` is a build file whose `resources` are
  the `flux-system/` dir plus `apps.yaml` and `infrastructure.yaml` — which are
  themselves Flux Kustomization CRs. The collision appears inside a single file.
- **`gotk-components.yaml` is truncated here.** The real file is ~35,000 lines of
  CRDs, RBAC and controller Deployments. This fixture keeps one `Namespace` and
  one `source-controller` Deployment as representatives.
- **Files that are YAML but NOT Kubernetes objects:** every `kustomization.yaml`
  (all seven of them) is a kustomize build file, not a stored API object. The
  `frontend-patch.yaml` overlays are partial Deployments — real `apiVersion`/
  `kind`, but incomplete documents that only mean anything after a merge.

## Open questions
- Given only a file's contents, how does a tool tell a
  `kustomize.toolkit.fluxcd.io` Kustomization apart from a
  `kustomize.config.k8s.io` one — is the `apiVersion` string the only reliable
  signal, and does the filename ever help or only mislead?
- Is a cluster root (`clusters/production/`) ever an editing target, given that
  everything in it either points elsewhere (`path:`) or was machine-generated by
  bootstrap?
- When `apps.yaml` sets `targetNamespace: production`, the namespace of the
  applied objects is decided in `clusters/`, not in `apps/`. Can you know an
  app's destination namespace by reading only `apps/production/`?
- `apps.yaml` `dependsOn` `infrastructure`, whose readiness is gated by a
  `healthChecks` entry pointing at a HelmRelease in another directory. Can a tool
  reason about a single directory's desired state without following those
  cross-directory dependency and health edges?
- The `flux-system/` GitRepository points `url` at
  `https://github.com/example-org/gitops.git` and `path` back at this cluster
  directory. If you only have the checked-out files, how do you know which
  directory is a reconciliation root versus a plain library of manifests?
